region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely consumed in Singapore as dim sum and hawker food
food_subgroup: Steamed rice noodle roll (cheung fun) with char siu (BBQ pork) filling, served with sweet dark soy sauce and sesame seeds; 1 portion approximately 130g (3–4 rolls)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific class for cheung fun (steamed rice noodle roll). FOODON_03315873 (rice noodle food product) is the closest defensible parent. A filled/rolled variant with char siu filling is not separately represented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodles — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for steamed rice noodle roll (cheung fun). No specific cheung fun code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for cheung fun. A0FJP (rice noodles) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for cheung fun or steamed rice noodle roll as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of cheung fun with char siu is approximately 130g (3–4 rolls), as commonly served at Singapore dim sum stalls and yum cha restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Cheung Fun with Char Siu — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for cheung fun with char siu. Values represent the composite dish including noodle sheet, char siu filling, and sweet soy sauce as typically served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodles and rice flour products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cheung fun with char siu found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated from rice flour noodle products in the GI database (rice noodles GI range 53–61). The tapioca starch component slightly moderates the GI relative to pure rice starch. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (60 × 42.9) / 100 ≈ 25.7, using 130g serving with 33g carbohydrate per 100g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sweet dark soy sauce (450mg sodium per 100g; approximately 585mg per 130g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side and use minimally, as the sweet dark soy sauce is the primary sodium contributor and a single serving can provide approximately 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~25.7) driven by large carbohydrate portion (43g per 130g serving) and added sugar from sweet soy sauce (7.5g sugar per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should be counselled that cheung fun has a moderate GI (60) but a high GL per serving (~25.7) due to the large rice noodle portion, and that the sweet soy sauce contributes an additional 10–15g of sugar per serving that is often underestimated; requesting sauce on the side and limiting portion size is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (450mg/100g) and low saturated fat (1.0g/100g); cholesterol 15mg/100g; overall fat content is low at 3.4g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cheung fun with char siu is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it a moderate cardiovascular concern primarily due to its sodium content from soy sauce; patients with CVD should limit sauce quantity and monitor overall sodium intake across the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheung fun with char siu (叉烧肠粉 / 叉燒腸粉) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish consisting of silky, translucent steamed rice noodle sheets rolled around barbecued pork (char siu), drizzled with sweet dark soy sauce and sesame oil, and garnished with sesame seeds. The noodle sheet is made from a batter of rice flour and tapioca starch, steamed on a flat tray or cloth to produce a smooth, slightly chewy texture. The dish is a staple of yum cha (dim sum brunch) in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Guangdong-style restaurants worldwide. Per 100g, cheung fun with char siu provides 190 kcal, 7g protein, 3.4g fat, and 33g carbohydrate, with 7.5g sugar largely attributable to the sweet soy sauce. Sodium is elevated at 450mg/100g (approximately 585mg per 130g serving) due to the soy-based sauce. The glycemic index is approximately 60 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard serving (~130g) is high at approximately 25.7, reflecting the large carbohydrate mass from the rice noodle sheets. A key clinical teaching point is that the sweet soy sauce is a hidden sugar and sodium source: patients with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension may not account for the sauce when estimating carbohydrate or sodium intake. Clinicians should advise requesting sauce on the side. The dish is dairy-free and relatively low in saturated fat, making it a moderate cardiovascular concern primarily through its sodium content. Cheung fun is named for its resemblance to an intestine (腸 = intestine, 粉 = noodle/powder) and is believed to have originated in Shaoguan, Guangdong province, China.
common_user_queries: cheung fun with char siu nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated from analogous rice noodle products in the University of Sydney GI Database, with the estimation basis clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cheung fun with char siu is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this composite dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB source for this dish). All core macros (protein_g 7.0g, fat_g 3.4g, carbohydrate_g 33.0g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate from rice noodle products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for cheung fun with char siu was found. GL per serving calculated as (60 × 42.9) / 100 ≈ 25.7 using 130g default serving. The input record had two default portions (130g and 180g); 130g is used as the primary serving size per the food name and HPB reference. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) has been replaced with the canonical HPB Singapore Food Composition Database URL as the authoritative approved source.

Added FoodOn URI FOODON_03315873 as closest defensible parent with gap note.
